questions.a: The decision-maker in reinforcement learning, e.g., a robot, AI, or software program.
Everything the agent interacts with or operates within.
A snapshot or representation of the environment at a specific point in time.
What the agent chooses to do in a given state.
A scalar signal from the environment telling the agent how good or bad its last action was.
The agent’s strategy: a mapping from states to actions (π(s) → a).
A full sequence of interactions from the start state to a terminal state.
Estimates how good a state or action is in terms of expected future rewards.
The expected return (total future reward) starting from state s and following the policy.
The expected return starting from state s, taking action a, and then following the policy.
The cumulative reward an agent expects to receive from a given point onward.
A number between 0 and 1 that reduces the value of future rewards to prioritize immediate ones.
A value-based RL algorithm that learns the optimal action-value function off-policy.
An on-policy RL algorithm that updates Q-values based on the actual action taken (State-Action-Reward-State-Action).
An extension of Q-learning using deep neural networks to estimate Q-values.
A policy-based RL method that directly adjusts the policy parameters to maximize expected reward.
Combines policy (actor) and value function (critic) for efficient learning.
An exploration strategy where the agent chooses a random action with probability ϵ and the best known action with 1−ϵ.
Selects actions probabilistically based on their estimated values using a softmax function.
Selects actions with the highest potential value considering both average reward and uncertainty.
Encourages exploration by adding a term to the objective that favors more stochastic policies.
